instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Tax-Saving Tips for a 10 Lakh Salary
Being a salaried employee can make you financially independent as you handle your and your family’s financial needs. However, when you earn an income, you are required to pay a tax on the same.
While paying income tax is important, there are ways in which you can minimise the tax liability. The Income Tax Act of 1961 lays down various provisions which, when used, can reduce the tax liability and enhance your savings. Let’s understand what these provisions include and how you can save taxes.
Tax-Saving Strategies for a ₹10 Lakh Salary
The tax liability depends not only on how much you earn, but also on the tax regime you choose and the deductions you claim during the year. Here are some smart tax-saving tips for 10 lakh salary
Choosing the Right Tax Regime
The first step in tax planning is deciding between the old tax regime and the new regime. This choice can significantly affect how much tax you pay on a ₹10 lakh salary.
The old tax regime offers different types of tax deductions and exemptions. It is generally suitable for you if you actively invest or incur expenses during the year that are eligible deductions. The new regime offers lower rates in income tax slabs but does not allow most deductions and exemptions. However, if you choose the new regime, income up to ₹12 lakhs would be tax-free. So, if your salary is ₹10 lakhs, you would have no tax liability under the new regime.
Maximise Section 80C Deductions
Section 80C allows salaried individuals to reduce the taxable income by up to ₹1.5 lakh under the old tax regime on eligible investments such as EPF contributions, ELSS funds, NPS, NSC, 5-year FDs, etc. Expenses like home loan principal repayment and life insurance premiums are also eligible for deductions under this section.
Using the full limit of Section 80C in a planned manner can help you claim the maximum deduction and enjoy tax savings.
Take Advantage of the HRA Exemption
Most of the companies offer House Rent Allowance(HRA). If you are living in a rented house and receive House Rent Allowance (HRA) as part of your salary, it’s possible to claim an exemption to reduce taxable income. The exemption is allowed under the old tax regime. To claim this deduction, you must maintain proper rent receipts and have a valid rental agreement. If the annual rent paid is more than ₹1 lakh, the landlord’s PAN must also be provided.
The HRA exemption is considered to be the lowest of the following
The HRA amount that you get
50% of your basic salary if you live in a metropolitan city, or 40%
Actual rent that you pay – (minus) 10% of your basic salary.
Let's assume you live in Mumbai, earn a basic pay of ₹60,000, receive ₹25,000 as HRA, and pay ₹22,000 as monthly rent. The exempt HRA would be ₹16,000 (rent paid -10% of basic salary), being the lowest of the aforementioned three values. The remaining ₹9,000 would be treated as taxable HRA.
Claim Section 80D Deductions on Health Insurance Premiums
Section 80D contained in the Income Tax Act allows deductions on premiums paid for health insurance plans and certain medical expenses, provided you opt for the old tax regime. It allows deductions on premiums paid for self, spouse, dependent children, and parents. A maximum deduction of up to ₹1 lakh is available under this section.
Utilise the tax deductions available on Loans
Loans taken for specific purposes can also provide tax relief. The interest paid on a home is eligible for a deduction under the provisions of Section 24(b), and the principal that you repay for the loan is allowed as an eligible deduction under the provisions of Section 80C within the ₹1.5 lakh limit. Interest paid on education loans for higher studies is deductible under the provisions of Section 80E without any maximum cap for up to 8 years.
Invest in the National Pension Scheme (NPS)
The National Pension Scheme (NPS) is a suitable option for you if you want to plan early for retirement while saving tax under the old tax regime. It is a pension plan backed by the Government of India. It allows small, regular investments that are allocated across equity and debt, helping build a retirement corpus over time. NPS investments up to ₹1.5 lakhs qualify for tax benefits under Section 80. Moreover, you get an additional deduction of up to ₹50,000 under Section 80CCD(1B).
Tax-Free Allowances
The Income Tax Act provides tax exemptions for specific allowances paid to employees to meet work-related expenses. These allowances, covered under Section 10(14), include travel, daily allowance, conveyance for official duties, uniform allowance, and allowances for academic or research activities. Fixed allowances are exempt only up to prescribed limits. If you qualify for this deduction, proper documents need to be maintained to claim this benefit.
How to Reduce Tax Liability on a ₹10 Lakh Income?
The final tax liability depends on factors such as the tax regime chosen, eligible deductions, and how salary components are structured.
Salary Structure
Salary structure directly affects how much tax is payable on a ₹10 lakh income. A higher basic salary increases tax liability, while properly structured allowances and contributions can lower taxable income. Components such as HRA, tax-free allowances, and retirement contributions help reduce taxable income and thus provide you with some extra money to spend on yourself now or save and invest for the future.
Old Tax Regime Strategies
If you choose the old tax regime, claim all the eligible deductions and exemptions from your taxable income. This can help in bringing down the net taxable income, and the tax payable also reduces.
New Tax Regime Strategies
If you choose the new regime, taxable income up to ₹12 lakhs enjoys zero tax liability. So, if you choose the new regime, a salary of ₹10 lakhs would give you a zero tax liability.
Tax Calculation Examples for Both New and Old Regimes
Riya earns ₹10 lakh annually and wants to minimise her tax liability in FY 2025–26. When she reviews the new regime, she finds that the standard deduction of ₹75,000 reduces her taxable income to ₹9.25 lakh. With the revised tax slabs and higher rebate limits, her income falls within the range where the rebate applies in full. As a result, Riya ends up paying no income tax, even though she has not claimed deductions for any tax-saving investments.
Now she reviews the old tax regime. Riya applies the standard deduction of ₹50,000 first, which brings her taxable income to ₹9.5 lakh. She then claims deductions of ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80C for tax-saving investments and claims ₹25,000 under Section 80D for health insurance. This lowers her taxable income to ₹7.25 lakh. While this significantly reduces her tax liability, it does not bring it down to zero.
For Riya, the comparison shows that the new regime offers immediate tax relief without needing to invest a single penny.
Key Takeaways
There are different ways to save taxes on a ₹10 lakh salary.
Deductions under Sections 80C, 80D, loan-related provisions, and HRA can significantly reduce taxable income under the old regime.
The new regime offers zero tax at this income level.
Health insurance, home loans, education loans, and retirement planning instruments like NPS serve a dual purpose by offering financial protection and tax benefits under the old tax regime.
Conclusion
If your annual income is close to ₹10 lakh, the new regime can make life easier. It allows you to enjoy zero tax liability without investing or claiming deductions. For many salaried professionals, this means higher disposable income to plan the future. So, plan your taxes efficiently to reduce the tax liability and enhance your savings.
FAQs
What are the major differences between the old regime and the new regimes?
The old tax regime allows different exemptions and deductions from your income, while the new regime has lower tax rates with fewer deductions. At a ₹10 lakh income, the new regime offers zero tax without any investments.
How can I maximise my tax savings with a salary of 10 lakhs?
You can maximise tax savings by choosing the right tax regime, optimising your salary structure, and using available deductions like Section 80C, 80D, HRA, and NPS if you opt for the old regime. Alternatively, the new regime may offer zero tax at this income level without requiring any investments.
How can I achieve zero tax liability on a ₹10 lakh salary?
You can achieve zero tax liability on a ₹10 lakh salary by opting for the new regime, where revised slabs and rebates can reduce tax to zero without needing any deductions or investments.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility

output.final_text:
Tax-Saving Tips for a 10 Lakh Salary
Being a salaried employee can make you financially independent as you handle your and your family’s financial needs. However, when you earn an income, you are required to pay a tax on the same.
While paying income tax is important, there are ways in which you can minimise the tax liability. The Income Tax Act of 1961 lays down various provisions which, when used, can reduce the tax liability and enhance your savings. Let’s understand what these provisions include and how you can save taxes.
Tax-Saving Strategies for a ₹10 Lakh Salary
The tax liability depends not only on how much you earn, but also on the tax regime you choose and the deductions you claim during the year. Here are some smart tax-saving tips for 10 lakh salary
1. Choosing the Right Tax Regime
The first step in tax planning is deciding between the old tax regime and the new regime. This choice can significantly affect how much tax you pay on a ₹10 lakh salary.
The old tax regime offers different types of tax deductions and exemptions. It is generally suitable for you if you actively invest or incur expenses during the year that are eligible deductions. The new regime offers lower rates in income tax slabs but does not allow most deductions and exemptions. However, if you choose the new regime, income up to ₹12 lakhs would be tax-free. So, if your salary is ₹10 lakhs, you would have no tax liability under the new regime.
2. Maximise Section 80C Deductions
Section 80C allows salaried individuals to reduce the taxable income by up to ₹1.5 lakh1 under the old tax regime on eligible investments such as life insurance premiums, PPF contributions, ELSS funds, NPS, NSC, 5-year FDs, etc1. Expenses like home loan principal repayment are also eligible for deductions under this section.
Using the full limit of Section 80C in a planned manner can help you claim the maximum deduction and enjoy tax savings.
3. Take Advantage of the HRA Exemption
Most of the companies offer House Rent Allowance(HRA). If you are living in a rented house and receive House Rent Allowance (HRA) as part of your salary, it’s possible to claim an exemption to reduce taxable income. The exemption is allowed under the old tax regime. To claim this deduction, you must maintain proper rent receipts and have a valid rental agreement2. If the annual rent paid is more than ₹1 lakh, the landlord’s PAN must also be provided2.
The HRA exemption is considered to be the lowest of the following2
● The HRA amount that you get ● 50% of your basic salary if you live in a metropolitan city, or 40% ● Actual rent that you pay – (minus) 10% of your basic salary.
Let's assume you live in Mumbai, earn a basic pay of ₹60,000, receive ₹25,000 as HRA, and pay ₹22,000 as monthly rent. The exempt HRA would be ₹16,000 (rent paid -10% of basic salary), being the lowest of the aforementioned three values. The remaining ₹9,000 would be treated as taxable HRA.
4. Claim Section 80D Deductions on Health Insurance Premiums3
Section 80D of the Income Tax Act,1961 allows deductions on premiums paid for health insurance plans and certain medical expenses, provided you opt for the old tax regime. It allows deductions on premiums paid for self, spouse, dependent children, and parents. A maximum deduction of up to ₹1 lakh is available under this section.
5. Utilise the tax deductions available on Loans4
Loans taken for specific purposes can also provide tax relief. The interest paid on a home is eligible for a deduction under the provisions of Section 24(b), and the principal that you repay for the loan is allowed as an eligible deduction under the provisions of Section 80C within the ₹1.5 lakh limit. Interest paid on education loans for higher studies is deductible under the provisions of Section 80E without any maximum cap for up to 8 consecutive years starting from the year of the first repayment.
6. Choosing National Pension Scheme (NPS)5
The National Pension Scheme (NPS) is a suitable option for you if you want to plan early for retirement while saving tax under the old tax regime. It is a pension plan backed by the Government of India. It allows regular investments that are allocated across equity debt, and other asset classes, helping build a retirement corpus over time. NPS investments up to ₹1.5 lakhs qualify for tax benefits under Section 80C. Moreover, you get an additional deduction of up to ₹50,000 under Section 80CCD.
7. Tax-Free Allowances6
The Income Tax Act provides tax exemptions for specific allowances paid to employees to meet work-related expenses. These allowances, covered under Section 10(14), include travel, daily allowance, conveyance for official duties, uniform allowance, and allowances for academic or research activities. Fixed allowances are exempt only up to prescribed limits. If you qualify for this deduction, proper documents need to be maintained to claim this benefit.
How to Reduce Tax Liability on a ₹10 Lakh Income?
The final tax liability depends on factors such as the tax regime chosen, eligible deductions, and how salary components are structured.
1. Salary Structure
Salary structure directly affects how much tax is payable on a ₹10 lakh income. A higher basic salary increases tax liability, while properly structured allowances and contributions can lower taxable income. Components such as HRA, tax-free allowances, and retirement contributions help reduce taxable income and thus provide you with some extra money to save and invest for the future.
2. Old Tax Regime Strategies
If you choose the old tax regime, claim all the eligible deductions and exemptions from your taxable income. This can help in bringing down the net taxable income, and the tax payable also reduces.
3. New Tax Regime Strategies
If you choose the new regime, taxable income up to ₹12 lakhs enjoys zero tax liability7. So, if you choose the new regime, a salary of ₹10 lakhs would give you a zero tax liability.
Tax Calculation Examples for Both New and Old Regimes
Riya earns ₹10 lakh annually and wants to minimise her tax liability in FY 2025–26. When she reviews the new regime, she finds that the standard deduction of ₹75,000 reduces her taxable income to ₹9.25 lakh. With the revised tax slabs and higher rebate limits, her income falls within the range where the rebate applies in full. As a result, Riya ends up paying no income tax, even though she has not claimed deductions for any tax-saving investments.
Now she reviews the old tax regime. Riya applies the standard deduction of ₹50,000 first, which brings her taxable income to ₹9.5 lakh. She then claims deductions of ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80C for tax-saving investments and claims ₹25,000 under Section 80D for health insurance. This lowers her taxable income to ₹7.25 lakh. While this significantly reduces her tax liability, it does not bring it down to zero.
For Riya, the comparison shows that the new regime offers immediate tax relief without needing to invest a single penny.
Key Takeaways
1. There are different ways to save taxes on a ₹10 lakh salary. 2. Deductions under Sections 80C, 80D, loan-related provisions, and HRA can significantly
reduce taxable income under the old regime. 3. Under the new tax regime, resident individuals can have zero tax on net taxable income up
to ₹12 lakh 4. Health insurance, home loans, education loans, and retirement planning instruments like
NPS serve a dual purpose by offering financial protection and tax benefits under the old tax regime.
Conclusion
If your annual income is around ₹10 lakh, the new tax regime can help you achieve zero tax liability, under the Section 87A rebate available for taxable income up to ₹12 lakh. The old
regime, on the other hand, allows various deductions and exemptions that can reduce taxable income and help you save tax, so choosing the right regime depends on which option lowers your overall tax outgo and
and enhances your savings.
FAQs
1. What are the major differences between the old regime and the new regimes?
The old tax regime allows different exemptions and deductions from your income, while the new regime has lower tax rates with fewer deductions. At a ₹10 lakh income, the new regime offers zero tax without any investments.
2. How can I maximise my tax savings with a salary of 10 lakhs?
You can maximise tax savings by choosing the right tax regime, optimising your salary structure, and using available deductions like Section 80C, 80D, HRA, and NPS if you opt for the old regime. Alternatively, the new regime may offer zero tax at this income level without requiring any investments.
3. How can I achieve zero tax liability on a ₹10 lakh salary?
You can achieve zero tax liability on a ₹10 lakh salary under the new regime, where revised slabs and rebates can reduce tax to zero without needing any deductions or investments.
